CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The' “ Empire’’ Music Hall, that was once better
known as Dan Lowry’s, is not in a prosperous state,
.and it would be unbecoming of us to shed crocodile tears
over its ill-fortune. We shut up one low music hall,
~ the Lyric, and we would not fret if the “ Empire’’ was
“ghut down also. Mr. Al Findlater was in the chair
at the twelfth ordinary general meeting, and the press
- was excluded. It appears that one Figgis, who was
manager of this place of “entertainment’’—and re-
freshment—swelled the tide of emigration by a human
unit. The man Figgis, it appears, left the music hall
with a large stain on his character: his defalcations
amounted to £884 8s. 1d. The man Figgis escaped;
he was an “Idolator ”’ he might have been made a
oral headline of in the pages of the libeller of Canon
MecInerney. s

We do not, know whether the man Figgis was a Free-
magon or not; but he got clear away anyway, and the

—==

shareholders can grin and bear it; we have no sym-
pathy to offer them. Mr. Al Findlater regretted to
state that the profits were “ practically £1,000 down ’’
on the year ; let us rejoice, as the street preachers might
say. Al complained of the difficulty of procuring what
ho called “ talent *’ at a reasonable figure. Why, there
is the keeper of the Magic Button, Mr. Daniel Tallon—
surely that wizard would be good “talent,’”” and he
might do a cheap “turn’’ to oblige a friend in “the
trade.”

The Dublin Castle Local Government Board has been
interfering, or trying to interfere, with the Limerick
Board of Guardians. The latter body resolved that
only materials of Irish manufacture be invited in the
advertisement for stationery contracts. After a dis-
cussion the letter was marked “read.” The Dublin
Castle Local Government Board can do a lot when all
the Elected Boards mark all its thunderbolts  read!”

The public, we feel sure, have been very pleased to
hear that the firm of O’Mara, of Limerick, have pur-
chased the Kechoe, Pakenham, and Donnelly business
The newspaper-reading public are familiar with the
main points of the‘decline and fall of the latter firm.
It would have been lamentable if, like the late tobacco
industry of Kennedy’s, this bacon factory was shut up,
and that Ireland, as a consequence, would have one
large manufacturing concern the less.  The force of
established reputation is a strong factor in business as
well as in politics; if certain men of established reputa-
tion are actively connected with a business enterprise
the public confidence is at once stimulated. ~Though
the ill-fated firm of Kehoe, Pakenham, and Donnelly
came to the ground, the fact that O’Mara’s, of Lime-
rick, have now taken over the enterprise naturally
causes the public to look forward to success, and that
public attitude when there is, as in this case, ample
justification for it, goes a long way in business  Alder-
man O’Mara states that the enterprise is to be carried
on as a local Dublin industry, and is not to be worked
as a mere part of the great Limerick firm associated
with his name. As a result of the various forces work-
ing in recent years in favour of Irish industry, Alder-
man O’Mara states that ‘the Irish Trade of his own
firm trebled within the past few years. That fact will
be very gratifying to our readers who, during the last
five years, have done such strenuous work in favour of
Irish industry. Messrs. O’Mara intend forming a pri-
vate limited company to work the Dublin industry they
have purchased, and though willing and able to provide
all the necessary capital, yet if any people who have
lost money in the old company and others interested in
the bacon trade, apply for shares their applications will
be considered.

It will be remembered that the wise Commissioners of
Rathmines by a vote of 8 to 5 decided that the Irvish
language is not a commercial subject. Well, if M.

Edmundson does not think Irish is a commercial subject,

his Dartry Dye Works thinks it is good enough for adver-
tising purposes. The Dartry Dye Works used Irish in
its advertisements. This point occurred to us at the
time we were commenting on the vote of the wise Com-
missioners, but some one unintentionally misled us by
tolling us that Mr. Edmundson was in the washing and
not in the dyeing line. Now if Irish is not a commercial
subject, was it in a spirit of anti-English sentiment that
the Dartry Dye Works adorned its advertisements with
that “uncommercial”’ language. Perhaps Mr. Ed-
mundson would tell us.

We have got some further information about this
Rathmines business that does not tally with the state-

82

ments of Mr. Edmundson no more than dods his view of
Irish as an “uncommercial ”’ subject tally with that
language being used for advertisirig putposes by a dyeing
company with which he is connected. The Technical
Instruction Committee unanimously recommiended that
Irish should form part of the curriculum of the Rath-
mines School of Commerce! Last year quite a large
number of persons applied to the Principal of the Rath-
mines School of Commerce that Irish should be taught.
The Principal very naturally brought the matter before
the members of the committee for their sanction to in-
clude the teaching of Irish in the scheol. After dis-
oussion, a vote was taken and the Irish was defeated by
the Chairman’s (Mr. Edmundson) casting vote, though
at the time the Dartry Dye Works (perhaps they ox-
clude green as an “uncommercial *’ colour) was adver-
tising in Irish.

Subsequently that decision, carried by the casting
vote of the highly commercial Mr. Edmundson, was
reversed, and a motion in favour of the teaching of Irish
was carried by a majority of two. At the June meeting
this year when the Principal brought up the school
syllabus for the approval of the commiftee, the inclusion
of Irish in it was wnanimously approved, and no less a
person than Mr. Edmundson himself was in the chair.

In view of the fact that the Technical Instruction
Committee unanimously approved of Irish, what business
has the Urban District Council to upset that decision?
Does the Technical Inmstruction Committee count for
nothing? And if it counts for nothing, what is the
meaning of allowing it to exist? If a solitary Sourface
applied to have the Zulu language taught, no doubt the
Ascendancy corner in the Rathmines Council would
stretch a point to oblige a friend and a brother. But
Trish, though useful for advertising a dye works with
which Mr. Edmundson is connected, is not a ‘‘com-
mercial’”’ subject. Perhaps Mr. Mayne or Mr. McBride
would bring up the matter again at the next meeting.

Mr. D. J. Bergin, corn factor, 131 and 135 North
XKing St., and 29 Smithfield, Dublin, sends us a post-
card which he received on September 19th, 1905, from
the Midland Great Western Railway Co. It is an ac-
count for goods consigned from Ballinasloe to the North
‘Wall—the goods were 32 bags of barley weighing 3
tons, 4 cwt., and the freight from Ballinasloe to North
Wall goods depot is £1 17s. 1d. Mr. Bergin writes:—
“Dear Sir—As an old reader, I would like to call your
attention to freights on the Midland Great Western
Railway. Now here is a small farmer living near Bal-
linasloe, who wishes to sell his little lot of 32 barrels of
barley in Dublin ; he sends it on to North Wall per this
line, but as if to penalise him for doing so, instead of
encouraging him, they charge him 37s. 1d. freight on
the 32 barrels, or about 1s. 2d. per barrel. Now this
is a monstrous charge, and comes specially heavy on the
poor man, who cannot send, at least, a waggon load.
No wonder you call them ‘ nation-killers.’ ’

The Freeman’s Journal did a lot to bring about such
Industrial Revival as there is! Didn’t it7% The Re-
vival is making a bit of a stir and the Freeman is rub-
bing its eyes wondering what it is all about. The lead-
ing article in its issue of September 20th, was entitled
“The Irish Industrial Revival.”” ’Twas well it woke
before it ate the blanket. When the Freeman opened
its eyes it put its foot in it. Iere is the firsh sentence
of its article:—* The future of Irish manufacture rests
with the Irish consumer.” Needless to say to our
readers who are thoughtful people that that oracular
pronouncement, of the Freeman is simply deplorable.
The future of Irish manufacture rests with a great many
people besides the consumer. Even the Freeman itself
as a consumer of printing paper cannot help Irish manu-
facture in that direction until some people inm Ireland
produce paper upon which it and all the other papers
may be printed. The LEADER as a consumer of paper,
“consumes ’’ Irish paper made at Ballyclare. Where
is the paper made that the Freeman “ consumes?’ To
be just to our contemporary, we understand that the
supply of Irish made paper is not equal to the possible

THE LEADER.

SepreEMBER 30, 1905.

domand, so that paper must be imiported; but this
concrete case, to go no further, shows the inanity of
the Freeman’s remark that “ the future of Irish manu-
facturo rests with the Irish consumer.” The future
of Irish manufacture rests with a complexity of men
and matters; it rests partly with the consumer, but it
also rests with the producer, with the railways and
other carrying companies, with primary, secondary, and
University education, with the Irish working man, and
with the Irish non-working man, both of the holding-up-
the-corner, and the eye-glass, cane and suede-glove
type, it rests with Bung, or should we say partly non-
Bung, it rests with the establishment of Tolerance,
Justice and Fair Play, it rests with the establishment
of Irish rule in Ireland, it rests with the reform of
worthless and anti-Irish landlords or their “ plantation’”
in some other unhappy country, it rests with the press,
with the politicians and many others. Now that the
Freeman ‘s rubbing its eyes concerning the Industrial
Revival, that started we understand in September,
1900, would it not be well if our ancient contemporary
would rub, or attempt to rub, the cobwebs off the
rafters of the department in which its brains are stored?

In the course of an article the  Irish '’ T'imes said : —
“ We view with suspicion the attempts that are being
made to compel the study of Irish, for we cannot help
thinking that they are often due—we do not say in
Mr. Oldham’s case—less to a desire to promote the
commercial welfare of Irishmen, than to endeavour to
set up an artificial barrier between Ircland and Eng-
land.”” What would Arnott’s paper think if we said
that one of the utilities of the Irish Language Revival
was the breaking down of the artificial conmection be-
tween England and its unfortunate prey, Ireland!

From a special article in the Scotsman of September
18th, headed “The Linguistic Survey of India,” we
take the following:—“ As a Government, we have at-
tempted everything, in the three centuries from Queen
Elizabeth to the Empress-Queen Victoria, to dispel the
mystery—the darkness—by light, to understand the

.soul of the people, their beliefs, their hopes, their fears.

Everything but one! The soul of a race is in its lan-
guage and literature, in its mother tongue and folk-
lore.”

The meeting against the Dublin Castle nominated
Commissioners of “ National’’ Education was a great
success; in many respects it was rather a surprising suc-
cess. Professedly the Gaelic League is National but
“ non-political,” and the “ non-political ’’ character of
the League was well emphasised in the personnel of the
speakers. The principal speaker after the President of
the League was Mr. Johu Dillon, a Parliamentary Party
leader. Mr. Dillon is a man who, according to his
lights, has given up his life to the service of his country,
and he is one for whom we have a real, though a much
qualified admiration. We wish he would interest him-
self in the intolerance and bigotry practised on the
Great Northern Railway Co., of which he recently was,
and we presume still is, a very large shareholder. Lord
Castletown of “ Boston speech *’ fame is Grand Secretary
of the secret society by the name of the Free and
Accepted Masons of Ireland, and fought, or at least did
militia duty, against the Boers in the recent iniquitous
war; Captain Otway Cuffe is a British officer. Now
wo take no exception to all this, for our position has
always been that the office of the Gaelic League is to
Irish the inhabitants of this country, to Irish the
tendnt and the landlord, the M.P. and the bailiff, the
gombeen man and the manufacturer, even ultimately to
Irish Alf Fox and Captain Wade Thompson of the
Porvort Pricsts Protection Society. Any people in Ire-
land who are against Irish Ireland must in the ordinary
way _be treated as hostile, but the ultimate objective is
to Irish all Treland. We think the Gaelic League acted
in the spirit of the Gaelic League in putting forwdrd
such_ meu as it did to speak, though whether the pro-
portions were properly kept may be a matter of divided
opinion ; whothier two speakers; one of whom took part
in the campaign against the Boers and is likewise Grand
Secretary of the Freemasons, and another who is &

|
|

 British officer, represented too heavy a lurch to one
side may fairly be reckoned an open question. ‘We see

~ that the President of the League took the Strokestown

branch to task for their recent resolution—a resolution
which 4Uf Foz, in its anxiety to hit the Gaclic League
by identifying it as a political body, seized upon like
a hawk. Of course nothing would serye the purposes of
Dublin Castle better than to be able to declare the
Gaell_c League po!i ical, and thereby have an oxcuse for
warning every civil servant and “ National”’ school
teacher off the ground. We are glad that the League
is alive to the danger of being dubbed an authentic
“ political * body, though there is no reason why the
pendulum should swing back too far. - It has been our
thankless office—it is our office, and it will be our office
—to do the unpopular but effective thing. The fact
that our warnings against the policy of ‘“confrering ”’
with any political party excited animosity against us in
some quarters is only a matter of detail;  the chief
thing is that the League is now alive to the pitfalls of
the situation in view of the fact that Dublin Castle, and
its jackals like 4/f Fox, are on the look-out to pounce
upon the League at the first opportunity.

Mr. Dillon in the course of his speech generously gave
the entire credit for bringing the matter of the con-
templated withdrawal of fees before the notice of the
Irish public to the Secretary of the League, who wrote
a letter which Mr. Dillon received on July 23rd,
drawing attention to Dr. Wilson’s speech in which that
“National *’ Commissioner announced the proposed
withdrawal of fees after June, 1906, Mr. Dillon, act-
ing on the request of the Secretary of the Gaelic League,
raised the question on the floor of the British House of
Commons on July 25th. We have observed, and our
readers no doubt have observed also, that for us is the
humble occupation of doing things, and for others, the
more exalted one, of receiving the credit. We take all
that as part of the day’s work. Was it the Freeman,
the Independent, the Irish Times, or the Skibbereen
Eagle that pioneered the Industrial Revival? In the
confusion we forget which; all we know is that the
“little > LeapErR had nothing to do with it! In the
““little >’ LEADER of June 24th, attention was drawn to
the proposed withdrawal of fees. We mention this fact
merely in the interests of accuracy; and Mr. Dillon will
be interested to hear that a public warning was sounded
in these columns upwards of a month before he re-
ceived the letter from the Secretary of the Gaelic League
requesting him to raise the matter on the floor of the
British House of Commons.

During the course of Mr. Dillon’s speech, he asked,
“ Why don’t they allow us to discuss it on the floor of
the House of Commons?’’ At this there were inter-
ruptions, Mr. Dillon very neatly silenced the opposi-
tion by remarking:—“I know there are men in this
hall who have a great contempt for discussion of that
character (applause). But permit me to say to them
that I only raised this question at the request of the
Gaelic League in the House of Commons (loud cheers).”’
It is evident that those who interrupted ought to turn
their resentment, not on Mr. Dillon, who only kindly
did what he was asked, but on to the Gaelic League and
its Secretary who requested him to bring up the matter
on the floor of the British House of Commons.

And now after the Rotunda enthusiasm—what next?
More enthusiastic meetings elsewhere? Tf enthusiastic
meetings work up a public opinion that will lead on
to public action, let us have as many of them as
possible; if they are mere occasions for blowing off
steam they are not only no use, but they act as safety
valves in the anti-Irish interest.

‘We think the needs of the time call for a combined
attack—as combined as may be—by the elected boards
on the nominated boards. Dublin Castle—including its
brat, Trinity College—will engage the enemy—that is
the Irish people—in detail if it can; it is for the people

~ to not allow.themselves to be taken in detail. Every

man with a cart should put his name in Irish, and in
TIrish alone, on it. Dublin Castle can seize the goods
of Alderman Cole, but it will find it difficult—with the

THE LEADER. 3 : 83

Trish people pecking at it at all sides—to seize the goods
of a fow hundred thousand cart owners. The defiance
which Mr. John Cummins, of New Ross, is showing to
the justly unpopular L.G.B. should be backed up l3y
every elected body having dealings with that Dublin
Castle Board. Whilst Dublin Castle is attacking
Boards of Guardians, Rural Councils should be attack-
ing Dublin Castle; the school manager and school-
masters should be snapping their fingers at the Com-
missioners of “ National’’ Education, and men of the
Mr. Sullivan stamp should be battering at the bigoted
gates of the Great Northern Railway, the Provincial
Bank, and the Royal Dublin Society. Indeed, the !(ey
of the whole battle is the fight for Tolerance, Justice,
and Fair Play. There are many blustering people who
will say anything these days but Sourface. Well, who-
ever may suffer from white liver, we still plod along
with our bell ringing up the people to tackle the anti-
Irish Sourfaces; even though Lord Castletown is a
Gaelic Leaguer—as he has a perfect right to be—
we are against the pernicious secret society—the Free-
masons of Ireland—of which he is Grand Secretary, and
of which Brother Goulding, “gentleman,’”’ is Inner
Guard Sword Bearer.

The conduct of the Commissioners of “ National
Education is so calculated to arouse angry passions in
the hearts of the people that perhaps it is just as well
that a jester in cap and bells should supply inimitable
comic relief by way of easing the tension. Our only
George, however, great his limitations in other re-
spects may be, makes a most excellent clown. Since this
gentleman turned “ Saved ’ he has done nothing better
in the broadly comic line than his recent offer to turm
voluntary schoolmaster—or find a substitute. — George
proved a great “slump ’’ in Ireland ; for though we are
a green people, we are not so superlatively verdant as to
be “taken in” by George; and even when he formally
and for advertising purposes left a religion in which he
had not believed, people naturally laughed. The sun,
as we all know, revolves round George, and it shows its
innate good breeding by keeping some 92,000,000 miles
away from No 4 Upper Ely Place, Dublin, for fear that
the spots on it might too grievously offend the keen artis-
tic sense of the author of “ Parnell and his Island.’

The only thing that we see out of harmony with the
fitness of things is that George followed Vesuvius in-
stead of Vesuvius waiting on the lead of George—
and Vesuvius might have had the good manners to give
precedence to an evangelical gentleman round whom the
son discreetly revolves at a civil distance. George is in
eruption some months after Vesuvius. We can see
George in our minds-eye boring his few remaining ac-
quaintances, and carving the air with his gestures—
“Good morning; are you on strike to-day?’ George
announces that he submits his name for what he calls
a “ voluntary reader,”” whatever that may be. We have
heard of “ Scripture readers’’ in the Irish-speaking dis-
tricts; and now that George is “ Saved ’’ he must ex-
pect—for we Irish are a suspicious people—that the
common Papist will wonder if George’s offer as a
“voluntary reader '’ has any connection with soup.
George makes an excellent clown ; but when it comes to
serious business his best friends should politely hint to
him not to walk on the grass.

 An Irish Irelander ’’ sends us a letter asking—Why
has Iroland no Mutual Insurance Company. It is a
pertinent question. We are flooded with British In-
surance Companies. In the course of his letter our
correspondent says:— Looking at the list of Scottish,
English, and other foreign Insurance Offices that ex-
ploit the whole field of Irish Life Insurance from centres
like Dublin, Belfast, Cork, and Galway, one cannot
help wondering how many Trvishmen do these concerns
employ at the head of affairs, and how much of the
high amount of premiums paid them in Ireland is in-
vested in this impoverished country, and not drained
away from it liko xent paid to the absentee landlords.
As your readers are aware there are two classes of In-
surance Companies—' Proprietary,” which has share-
holders to divide the profits with the policy-holders at a
certain, usually fixed, ratio. These profits paid to shaxre-

holders in the case of some of the companies draining
- Ireland amount to a fabulous sum as compared with
the capital at first invested by the stockholders. ~The
other class of company is ‘ Mutual,’ formed in this
manner—a number of people originally met, and having
decided to start the company, filled in Lheir_proposa.ls
for assurance in it, and deposited their premiums, and
thus formed the nucleus of the necessary funds to go on
with. The whole of the profits of this moro up-to-date
class of insurance concern are divided amongst tho
policy-holders.”’ Perhaps some of our r(_mderg who are
in the insurance line would make a mova in this matter.

"The run on the  Philosophy of Irish Ireland’’ has
been so rapid that at this carly date we arc obliged to
announce the volume out of print.  Messrs. Dufly,
Messrs, Eason, Messrs. Gill, and some other agents
throughout the country - may have copies
“still  unsold, but the printers have no
more on hands, and the sale is stopped at this
office. Owing to the great success of the republication
of these essays it has occurred to us that a reprint of a
collection of some of the editorial articles in the early
numbers of the LEaDER would be welcomed by the
thoughtful public, and would serve a useful purpose at
the present time. Many of our present-day readers
never read the earlier issues of the LEADER, and young
men of twenty years of age who are now taking a keen
interest in Irish affairs were only school boys of fifteen
when the LEADER started. The LEADER had a relatively
new national gospel to preach, and it was for that pur-
pose it was founded. So much was this so that besides
the notes on current affairs the editor wrote no less than
thirty-nine articles in the first six numbers, that is an
average of six articles in each nwmber, and three over.
There were a lot of things to be said of a foundation-
laying order five years ago, and we think they would
bear re-saying in the form of a booklet five years after-
wards. Already, as our readers know, a collection from
the famous TIrish articles contributed to the LEADER by
Father Peter O’Leary, of Castlelyons, has been pub-
lished by the Irish Book Company. If we bring out
the collection of editorial articles from the earlier issues
of the LEADER to which we have been referring, a further
collectioa of the articles by “ Imaal’’ and others who
did so much to make the great reputation of the LEADER
might follow.

The Rector of University College, Dublin, has written
a letter to the Mayor of Limerick concerning “ M.
O’R.,” the new Rector of the Irish College at Rome.
In the course of his letter Rev. Dr. Delany, S.J.,
writes :— Though unconnected with Limerick, I ask
your leave to associate myself with the objects of the
meeting over which you presided so worthily, and I
shall be grateful to be permitted to join with the citi-
zens of Limerick in their testimonial to the Very Rev.
Dr. O'Riordan. His claims on the gratitude of Irish
Catholics are not bounded by the limits of the city or
county of Limerick. By his recent admirable work in
which, out of the mouths of hostile witnesses, he so
triumphantly refutes the traducers of our Catholic re-
ligion and the Irish people, he has laid under a deep
obligation the Catholics, not of Limerick alone, but
throughout the length and breadth of Ireland, and be-
yond it—wherever in any land the Catholic feels con-
cerned for the good repute of his religion, or the TIrish-
man who fecls sensitive to slander upon his race. In-
debted as I feel personally for the special service Dr.
O’Riordan has done for the cause of education, and
admiring as I do the marvel of whole-souled dovoted-
ness which alone could have produced sucli a work from
the pen of a hard-working city curate in indifferent
health, I am impelled to ask leave to join with the
people of Limerick in their tribute of admiration and
respect ; and I shall be grateful if you will be pleased to
accept the enclosed offering for that purpose.’

The Pembroke Technical Schools will reopen on Mon-
day, October 2nd. A feature of the new Session con-
sists of the establishment of a Trade Preparatory De-

artment in the Ringsend school. We learn that this
is an innovation in Dublin, but such schools have al-
ready been successfully established in other parts of

THE LEADER. !

SEPTEMBER 30, 1905.-

TIreland. The object of a Trade Preparatory School is to
‘enable boys who have already passed successfully

through the sixth standard of a National School, or its

cquivalent, to devote some years to a special course of
studies, which will fit them, after they have passed
through an apprenticeship, for positions of responsi-
bility in industrial life. In similar schools elsewhere,
it is found that local employers are most anxious to
secure the pupils as apprentices, and as many of the
leading employers in Dublin have informed the Pew-
broke Technical Instruction Committee, that they are
in  sympathy with the establishment of their
School, 1t is believed that it is more than prob-
able that boys who have passed through it will find
themselves favourably situated, as regards the obtain-
ing of employment. Motor Engineering is also receiv-
ing special attention; new and well equipped motor
workshops have been added, and arrangements have been
completed by which students can make themselves
thoroughly competent motor-car drivers. These who
are interested in the application of motor propelled
vehicles to commercial purposes, will find an oppor-
tunity of hearing a special lecture to be given on this
subject by a well-known authority, Mr. E. Shrapnell

A REASON FOR A BETTER CONCEIT OF
OURSELYES.

OME of the Scots are said to have prayed to the
N Lord to give them a good conceit of themselves;
it 1s a thing we Irish have need to pray for, too—more
need for than the canny Scots ever had. Historical
causes, of course, drove us out of heart, but the suc-
cess of O’Connell with the Catholic Question, and of
Parnell with the Land Question, showed what could
be done by organisation. Out of organisation we got
some courage, but we want more. We got a kind of
collective courage, but we want individual grit, and
we had better get it as fast as we can.

We have allowed ourselves to be depressed to an in-
ordinate extent, utterly beyond what the circumstances
warrant, but, of course, circumstances bore hardly
against us. We have had to struggle not only against
a foreign power, but against a Plantation of that
power. The Plantation, more anti-Irish than the abso-
lute foreigner, has propagated the doctrine of the in-
feriority of the “ native Irish’’ (as if there were any
foreign Irish!) con amore, and in the absence of tangible
proofs to contradict his pretensions, he practically held
the field. Men like O’Donovan, Petrie and O’Curry
did great things towards rescuing the pre-Norman
period of Ireland from the extravagant disdain of the
aliens, but the Plantation held fast by its established
prestige in everything not susceptible of, or else not
subjected to, a definite test, and one of these things
was education. Nobody in Ireland was supposed to
be “educated ’’ except the Plantation, and such of the
“ native Irish *’ as had been to Trinity College! There
was no free field of open competition where the general
educational merits of the secondary schools could be
testod, and as the Plantation had an immense start of
us in all facilities for education, the said Plantation
openly assumed its own ubter superiority, and many
of ourselves silently acquiesced in the assumption—cer-
tainly feared its being true. But the Intermediate
Education systom and the Royal University were estab-
lished, and a mode was so provided of testing what the
secondary schools and seminaries were doing. How did
the Catholics emerge from that test? Let the results
of the examinations of both bodies, the Intermediate
and the Royal University, answer that! Had we
cause for such a poor opinion of ourselves after all?
Four years ago, when the Intermediate published its
Prize Lists for what was supposed would be the last
time, I dealt with this aspect of the matter, so that I
need hardly deal with it again. Were there any need
to do so, I could do it with a light heart. The Inter-
meodiate Board has resumed publication of its Prize
Lists, and the same results are shown now as in 1901.
The people who modestly lay claim to the wealth and

intelligence of the country—or, at least, to five-sixths
of it—are still whete they wers thedl, only more o, if
anything. The figures already quoted in the editoridl
notes of this review retider it needless for ine to go ito
the statistics of the Intormediate results ini any detail;
T have another purpose, and shall pass on to ib.

intend to deal with one particular argument which
those of the other side put forward in their defence
when dealing with this (for them) awkward question.

A large proportion of the Catholic successes have been
won by the Christian Brothers, and this has been always
so. But what difference doos this make in the matter?
None at all, to my mind; yot it is one of the very
‘first things brought up by non-Catholics in discussing
this question of the relative merits of Catholic and Pro-
testant schools and students in Ireland. Why? Well,
you see, the Christian Brothers educate boys very inex-
pensively; they are celibates, with vows of poverty,
living in community. You sce, it is a sort of “ Chinese
cheap labour,” an under-cutting of the market rates,
and, somehow, it’s not playing the game fair! They
can sweep the field by sheer force of numbers, and so
on; their cheap scale of fees enables them to draw upon
a larger class of the community. How this should pre-
vent the other side from showing their  five-sixths of
the intelligence ’—if they really have it—I cannot see;
yet it somehow does, for it takes them all their time to
get one-third of the exhibitions, and they would not
have got one-third, even, had the Commissioners not
arbitrarily docked a large number of exhibitions off the
total usually awarded.

But the Chinese cheap labour’ argument cannot
apply to the Convents. These establishments are
mostly boarding-schools, and their students cannot be
said to be “poor girls,” as the boys of the Christian
Brothers are constantly spoken of by the other side as
“ poor boys.”” How is it that the Convents, these nigri
Zocz, win twelve Senior Grade exhibitions, and the non-
Catholics only one? How it is, for instance, that St.
Louis Convent, Monaghan, which is mainly a boarding-
school, comes out higher than the two best Protestant
girls’ schools, Victoria High School, Derry, and Vic-
toria College, Belfast, which are mainly day schools?
There is no use in inventing “ Chinese cheap labour ”
arguments to explain away honest victories. How is it
that Clongowes, and St. Colman’s College, Fermoy,
both boarding-schools—the  former exclusively so—
come out higher than the best of the Protestant
schools, which are mainly day schools? If the “celi-
bate”’ argument be pleaded against the Catholic
schools, the answer is that many of the Protestant
schools are endowed, so that one thing balances the
other.

There is another point to which I shall draw atten-
tion ; the success of the Presbyterian as compared with
the Episcopalian Protestant schools. In the case of the
boys’ schools this is espeeially noticeable.  The old
Royal Schools and Grammar Schools have got a sad
battering ; there are eighteen schools which make a bet-
ter performance than the High School, Dublin (one of
the Erasmus Smith schools), and twenty-eight which do
better than the once-proud “ Portora’’ Royal School
at Enniskillen. Schools, of course; have their fluctua-
tions of success, but on the whole, the Presbyterian
schools seem to hold their own more uniformly than the
Episcopalian. In the case of the girls schools a similar
result is to be noted, that is, that the Protestant schools
which did best were those in the most Presbyterian part
of Ireland. Are the Episcopalians of the Ascendancy
getting dead beat? Looks like it. Is this why Trinity
College wants to get new blood into it, oven from the
“Romanists ¥’  Or from the Presbyterians?!  Looks
like it!  But more of that some other time. Mean-
while, I say, let us take unto ourselves a good conceit!
‘We have the most successful boarding-schools, the most
successful day schools, sixty-seven per cont. of the ex-
hibitions, a commanding lead in the highest grade

- (notably amongst the girls), and no sign of recession

from the position of 1901, emigration notwithstanding.

Isflf literary methtods of Seumas MacManus.
There is nothing strange about ‘this ; they have been for

Milligan cries out for' realism as if in justifying the ways
and moans of the art of Mr. MacMafius, she justifies i
roalisth ; ot, as if, on the other Hand, in justifying : |
realism sho justifies somic very homely stories of Mr. |
MacManus’s. s work is thus cogplqd with realism—
arid bhis sets one thitking of what realism may bf’-
Again; having laid down the law of rcalism unto her-
solf; Miss Milligan thinks that necessarily she must ac-
copt Paddy and the Pig, and all the many. other out-
rageous * tradesmiarks 7 of 'Irelam_d. True, there are ¥
Paddys in Tveland, likewise I’l'gs, and frequently
it happens that the pigs are driven by the Paddys
past milestones along roads, even along the roads
to Cork—ergo, these dusty, but cheerful, wayfarers must
tramp through the pages of novels bh;l,b set out to deal
with the real life of Ireland. Such sights are really to
be seen on the roads, therefore they must come into
realistic books, the realist priditig himself not so much on
his visionary powers as on his powers of vision. The
realist must put everything—good, bad, and indifferent
—_that he seés with his corporal eyes on the canvas, pigs :
as well as angels, and slime as well as stars, mixing thgm |
all “ promiscuous like "’ even as they are mixed “ with
rocks and stones and trees’’ on this poor old world of
ours. This is the ordinary conception of realism; it is 1
the generally accepted meaning of the term. Let us en- 4
deavour to find out whether realism is real, whether it
is true, whether it presents the whole truth, or only the
partial. 3 ; .
When Romeo, in the vault of Juliet, having slain
Paris, settles himself to die, he make a tremendously
fine speech stored with such “conceits ”’ as would be
found only in Elizabethan drama. It contains the well-
known words, “beauty’s ensign yet is crimson in thy
lips and in thy cheeks,”” etc. ; and further on we have:—

“Shall I believe
That unsubstantial death is amorous;
And that the lean abhorréd monster keeps
Thee here in dark to be his paramour?”’

With much more of this true lyric richness. Now, a
realist would never have done this. Such a passage
would be labelled as sentimental, or perhaps, imaginary.
And so Shakespeare and every poet that ever wrote are
knocked out of court at once. Realism, be it minded, i
deals with real life. The poets deal with life as they %
see it with that inner eye of theirs Realism would not
certainly, even were it able to write or think it, put 3
that speech into Romeo’s mouth at such a time; it prob-
ably would have kept him for the most part tongue-tied,
fearful of interription, gasping and staring; finally it-
might even make him, having no respect for the heroics,
get very red in the face even as Louis X VI. did just be:
fore his execution. Which now is the better way, the
realist’s or Shakespeare’s?

Take another case. A well-beloved young and beau-
tiful queen might be slaughtered in the same manner as
a robber. To the corporal eye things happened much
the same. But what a difference in the thoughts of the
spectators| Now the realist leaves these thoughts, emo-
tions, out of consideration ; they certainly are not fixed
quantities; neither aro they visible; but who doubts
their existence; aie they not real, as real as the bodily
petsons of the actors ‘in thie incident? This, then, is
our complaint agdinst rdalism—that it is not real ; that
it gives only the partial truth, not the whole truth.: The
whole truth to 6ur mind consists of & ropreseutation of
both the inicident and the mood, wedded as they are in
life. Realism omits the mood. -And so we would ex-
ouse that long swansong of Romeo’s as an index, not
only of the thoughts that might pass through the brain
of a romantic young man on such an occasion, but also
as an index of the thoughts of the spectator. Such werds
a8 these creato the mood in which we would be likely to
have witnessed’ the incident in real life, given that we
kngw tho fore-part of the story.

Artistry tends more and more to._elaborate the mood,
famish the incidenf, until finally we arrive ab the
Nhistler stage in painting where men and women,
xivers, bridges, shops and ships, are no longer things
that have substance ; they have become silver-greys and
puple—bluesfharmonles that delight the eye, that live
and ha:ve their being in the eye, but not in that inner
«eye which cannot be satisfied with anything less than the
whole truth. The incident, has, in these pictures, faded
out. Have we then the mood? No, wo have only the
keynotes of the mood, or, as I might say, we have the
symbols of the mood, not, bo it remembored, the sym-
bol.s of the incident which would be legitimate art, and
‘quite satisfying to the inner eye. Tho symbols of life,
«of incident, are quite legitimate art because they have
JPower to excite a mood as well as being beautiful in
‘themselves.  The symbols of the mood have not this
power, and so, though beautiful in themselves, fall
short of being of the nature of art.
To make all this simple—As I writo this I think of
evening because evening is at hand. I try to analyse
my thoughts, my emotions. They are a curious
blend. Angelus-bells and factory-horns, prayers and
the ceasing of work, social gatherings round homely
lamps, starry spaces and still waters, hedge-rows and
flying bats, with whirring little things that crawl about
—these in glimpses and remembered sounds enrich my
-conception. I know not the beginning of my thought or
its end. I only know that it is rich and satisfying.
Any piece of artistic endeavour that brings me to the
same condition of being, whether it be in the way of
written or spoken words, or musical sounds, or sculp-
tured or coloured form, I count legitimate and good art.
It satisfies my senses; it fills my inner eye. I have the
incident before me; and at the same time the mood is
created for me under the influence of which this incident
is to be witnessed if it is to be memorable and beautiful.
This is for me the true realism, and I find the poets to
be the true realists. Keats’s “ Ode to Autumn *’ will
:serve as an example of what I mean—a poem in which
both the delineation of the subject and the creation of
mood, which was itself originally created by the subject,
are beautifully balanced, each assisting, explaining, en-
riching the other.
Now, turning back to Whistler. I cannot but sup-
pose that his remembered impressions of eventide were
richer, fuller than mine. He put by the mood, as a
thing to be contemned, hidden. But the purples and
the blues and the golds and silvers—these, the concomi-
tants of evening, the symbols of the mood (not the sym-
bols of evening, evening requiring symbols more human
“than colours) he loved, lingered over, and painted. We
behold ; the eyes are gladdened, but the spirit is not
refreshed. Much the same fault, thinness, I find in the
poetry of A. E., though I am not acquainted with
much of it. Artistry, no longer beholding a subject, be-
holds its own countenance in every well ; it is enamoured
.of its own beauty and of the beauty of its workings on
the mind of man. This kind of art is as far from
.realism as that which breaks away thus from the past—

Man, bird, beast, are but beast, bird, man—
Simply themselves, uncinct by dower

Of dyes which, when life’s day began,
Round each in glory ran.

“True realism ought to recognise that the “ dyes’’ are as
-real as the things they make iridescent.

It may be somewhat premature to discuss realism in
Trish or in Anglo-Irish literature to-day ; it is certainly
rather extraordinary to discuss it in connection with
the writing of Seumas MacManus, and it as cortainly
has, of its own nature, less to do with pigs than with
metaphysics.  Still Mr. Yeats and his fellows have
‘taught every, or almost every, birdling on the bough to
-sing of the Well at the World’s End and the Flush of
the Purple Peace. That Well is not deep, nor the
Hush lasting. Then will the self-same pens dip them-
gelves in horrid, raw realisms—with memories of Rus-
sian and English and French writings breaking in on

~ HE immigration from Scotland and England into
’I Ulster did not cease with the “ Plantation.”
It continued and even went on increasing till about the
year 1700. The Lowlands of Scotland supplied most of
the settlers, but quite a variety of nationalities were
represented in the stream. MacIntosh (“ Making 'of
Ulsterman ”’) says of them:—* The name of this Irish
province was spread over Kurope by the second decade
of the 17th century as the ¢ Shelter of the Hunted,” and
soon Puritan and Quaker are joined in Ulster by an-
other nobleman of God’s making—the Huguenot from
France.  Headed by Louis Crommellin they come a
little later, and settle in and around Lisburn. And
still later, following the ‘Immortal William,” came
strong, brave Burghers from Holland and the Nether-
lands. Thus Ulster became the gathering ground for
the very finest, most formative, impulsive and aggres-
sive of free, enlightened, God-fearing people of
Europe.”

Leaving, for clearness, the adjectives out, this gives
an idea of the composition of the immigrant stream.
As to census, although the plantation was surveyed
more than once, I don’t know that we have any very
clear summary of the number of settlers. Different
authorities estimate them variously. Hanna mentions
lcosely that ““high rents drove the Scotch to Ireland at
the rate of 4,000 a year.”” Considering that Petty in
1672 estimated the whole population of Ireland at
1,100,000—(800,000 Irish, 200,000 English, 100,000
Scotch)—this would appear to be an exaggeration—as
regards the whole period under review, at least. Later
on, Synge estimated that, from the Revolution (1690)
to 1715, 50,000 families had settled in Ulster.”’
On the whole, and bearing in mind the total popula-
tion of the island at the time, it will be evident that
enough strangers came into Ulster to decidedly colour
that province.

Let me now quote one or two writers as to the state
of things in TUlster consequent on these happenings.
Though driven to “bog, forest, and mountain,’”’ the
native Irish had still to be reckoned with. “ Still, in
all these cases,”” says Davis (‘Patriot Parliament ’)
“the bulk of the population remained on the soil. To
leave the land was to die. They clung to it amid suf-
ferings too shocking to dwell on ; they clung to it under
such a serfhood as made the rapacity of their con-
querors interested in retaining them on the soil. They
clung to it from necessity and from love. They multi-
plied on it with the rapidity of the reckless. Yet they
retained hope—the hope of restitution and vengeance.’”
Chichester (to Salisbury, 1610) has a way of his own for
saying the same thing. ‘For they,”” he writes, “are
content to become tennantes to anie man rather than
be removed from the place of their byrth and education,
hopyinge, as I conceive, att one tyme or other to finde
an opportunitie to cutt their landlords’ throats.”” And
again:—“TI will do my best to prevente their revolte,
but I greatly doubt yt, for they ave infinitely discon-
tented. We are now, all of us, become buylders and
planters here, and not wasters and destroyers as in our
younger yeares, and would gladly rest in quiett if our
yll noighbours wyll permitt us; and that malkes us the
more sbudious to prevent their revolt, and to settle in
peace and quietness amongst them.”” One more quota-
tion on this head. In Pynnar’s Survey, 1619, is this
observation :—“I observe the greatest number of Irish
to dwell upon lands granted the City of London, whose
agents, finding the Irish more profitable than the Bri-
tish tennants, are unwilling to draw on the British.”’

So much for the old Irish; now for the Scotch. T
have already, at the head of this chapter, given
Hanna’s record of contemporary opinion of them; T -
may give here an estimate or two more. Stewart, the

son of one of the ministers who came over, says:—
From Scotland came many, and from England not a
few, yet all of them, generally the scum of both na-
« tions, who, from debt, or breaking or fleeing from jus-
tice, or seeking shelter, came hither, hoping to be with-
.out fear of man’s justice, in a land whero there was
nothing or but little, as yet, of the fear of the God.
- . . On all hands Atheism increased, and disre-
gard of God; iniquity abounded with contention, fight-
ing, murder, adultery.”” C. G. Duffy in quoting this
defends the settlors against their own minister. “In
many cases,”” he writes, ‘it is probable they were good
soldiers or skilful husbandmen, who, under more favour-

those whom Divine Proviednce did send to Ireland there

THE POLITICIANS AND THE UNIVERSITY
3 GRIEVANCE:

€ HE English Government take no notice of an
Trish grievance until things are made hot for
ib; and the Irish powers that be—the politicians and
the Press—will go on ignoring this national movement
until it becomes so pressing that they will have to swal-
low it whether they will or no.”” = These words seem
capable of a wider application than that originally in-
tended by the brilliant author of “7'%e Philosoply of
Irish Ireland,” and to make that application to the
University Question in Ireland is the object of the pre-
sent writer. The condition of University education in
Treland to-day constitutes a grievance, and more than a
grievance ; it is a crime committed against the country.
But as things have not been made hot for the Govern-
ment, no notice is taken of this Irish grievance.  The
question has not yet been made a pressing one, it is
practically ignored by Press and politicians alike.
The politicians are the self-acknowledged leaders of
the people. ~ What have they done?  Most of their
“ work ’ on this question has been done in Parliament.
We are taught to be always in a state of expectation
as to the great and glorious things Parliament will do
for us—" at no distant date.”” Up to the present, at
least, their “ work ” has not borne much fruit. The
M.P’s. have practically confined themselves to the an-
nual sham battle, called “a full-dress debate,” on the
University Question. The last hardy annual may have
been a full-dress debate, but it was a very poor affair
from the Irish point of view. The speeches of the Irish
members were for the most part feeble, and lacking in
a real understanding of the wrong done to the country.
.Qur M.P’s. may have caught the House of Commons’
manner in their presentation of this question before
Parliament, but a body of opinion is growing up in Ire-
land on this question which refuses to be charmed by
the languor and urbanity of their representatives.
Many are saying that this sort of perfunctory business
unpleasantly reminds them of tho tactics and the use-
less talk of Butt and his followers. They are saying,
too, that the Irish members have apparently not taken
the trouble to understand this question, which is a mat-
ter of life or death for the nation. They are saying,
moreover, that if our representatives were in earnest
about this question they could very easily find means of
making it hot for the Government. Were they, for
.example, to exhibit a healbhy curiosity abouf all public
money voted for Higher Education in any part of the
sister kingdoms, and to oppose all such grants to the
utmost limits allowed by the rules of the Iouse, they
.could very easily make the giving of a proper system of
Higher Education to this country a burning question.
This cold-blooded denial of our rights is causing tho
nation deadly harm; a sense of indignant anger at this
iniquitous wrong has taken possession of many up and
down through the country; and if our representatives
can’t, or won’t, make this a burning questim for the
House of Commons, then there are some of us who
fail to see what good they are doing, or what they are

_there for. It is plain to the meanest intelligence that

“the English Parliament will do nothing for University
Education in Ireland until it is forced to do it. We
.expect our M.P’s. to do their share in that forcing. If

THE LEADER.

SEPTEMBER 30, 1905.

they refuse, then as soon as our people awake to ‘the
magnitude of the issues involved, they will compel their
ropresentatives to lay aside their languor, to to step
down and out. A%

The newspapers, too, are, for the most part, just as
fooble and incfficient. They give reports of meetings
on the University Question; but they do not keep at
tho question—they do not seck to educate and to form
public opinion on the matter. ~ They publish articles
now and then on this subject, but as a rule they are
jejune, nerveless, pointless, passionless. A stranger
reading them would be inclined to say—“Oh! I am
glad it is no worse. I thought from what some of them
wore saying that the University Question in Ireland was
a serious matter, but when this able editor can only
make out a case as weak as this, of course, there’s no-
thing in it.””  The present writer once saw an apt
comment upon such an article in one of our daily papers.
On the side of the paper which contained the article,
some sarcastic person had written in coloured pencil
the following dialogue:—

Editor” (looking through window, and evidently suf-
fering from brain fag, to small boy in the street)—
“Hello! what are you doing there?”’

Small Boy—* Miching.”’

Editor (eagerly)—‘ What book are you in?’

Small Boy— Fourth.”’

Editor— Oh, that wfil do! Come in and help me.
to write this article on University Education.”

Non talt auzilio. You cannot expect success
from such advocacy. It looks as if there
is only one way of gaining our rights on this
matter. The masses of the people must, in

some way, be instructed on this question. Tt is a

roundabout way, but it may prove to be the shortest

way home. It would require a good deal of hard work

to bring home the salient points of this question to the

understanding of the people. It is a roundabout way;

but seeing that so many direct methods, and all-of-a

sudden plans, have been tried and failed, this method
deserves, at the least, an honest trial. Other methods

have been tried, and have failed—there’s no doubt

about that. Isolated meetings have been held; we

have had articles, programmes, editorials, speeches,

and resolutions on the subject attaining to quite edify-
ing dimensions. The quality of the output, however,

can scarcely be said to bear any satisfactory relatiou to
the quantity thereof. Then we have had a Royal Com-

mission on the subject; this statesman and that states-
man have been approached, and their words and pro-
mises duly reported; the annual manceuvres of our
M.P’s. take place with becoming regularity and de-
corum in the House of Commons; and the net result of
it all is that we seem to be farther away than ever from
wringing out of our rulers this undeniable right of
a free people—the right to a suitable system of
Higher Education for the people of this land. We
have failed so far, because we have neglected to arouse
and make use of the greatest force in Ireland to-day—
the onslaught of public opinion against intolerable op-
pression and wrong. Such efforts as have been made
to gain University Education for this country have
hitherto been made in a haphazard way, without any
organisation or unity of purpose. We want a common
plan for action which will make an army of our volun-
teers.  Random-firing from scattered ranks means
wasted effort. We want action, we want united action,
and we want it now. It is the firm belief of one, at
least, that thero are forces in Ireland to-day which, if
co-ordinated and brought into play, would soon compel
a solution, and win a satisfactory system of Higher
Education for Treland. ¢

N my previous article on the proposed election of an
1 Tnish Academy I suggested that the Coisde Gnotha
would do well to postpone the matter for a few years.
The time is not ripe for a step of this kind, which by
being inopportune would be worse than useless. I

showed that the fact of election by a body constituted
as the Coisde Gnotha would bring no honour or dis-
tinction to any man of established reputation, and such
-an election would place those whose reputation is not
established in a very unenviable position.  To be
elected as an Aca@emicizm for eminence in a language
by a body, two-thirds of whom may be said not to be
able to speak or write the language, is not a distinction
o be coveted. There is really time enough for reaching
by gradual stages the Academy terminus. I trust,
thereforc, the Coisde Gnotha will be wise in time and
refrain from this election at present.  Should other
:counsels prevail, however, and the election be proceeded
with, the great difficulty of taking liberties with in-
«dividual scholars in putting forward their names for
election must surely suggest to the membors of the
Coisde Gnotha some more reasonable solution of the ques-
tion.  The Academy in the beginning should consist of
only 6 or 7 members, and it should not be difficult to
select that number by an open unanimous vote. Per-
sons should be chosen whose names would surprise no-
body, names favourably known to the public and to the
Trish race of every clime as writers and scholars in the
Irvish language, men whose names will carry weight in
the councils of the learned, men whose learned and
literary productions are in the hands of Irish students
the world over, men who have a natural grip of the
language of the Gael.

As a qualification for Academic honours, I should
regard the being an Irish speaker as essential, but it
does not take one very far; to be an Irish speaker and
an Irish writer of power is better still, but even these
two qualifications combined fall a good deal short of
what should be required of every Academician. There
are the additional qualifications of solid learning in the
Irish language and Irish literature, and a general edu-
cation of the University type. But these qualifications
in the case of Academicians should not be proved merely
from hearsay. In an age like this palpable public evi-
dence in the shape of published books, etc., must be
Fforthcoming. Indeed, rightly understood, a distinction
like membership of an Academy worthy of the name
should be as it were the crowning honour of a life of
learned and literary labour, and is more suited for
those already advanced in years who have, so to speak,
made our book-shelves groan with their works; than for
the young, who may have done good work, but who are
not yet past the examination stage, the greater part of
whose work is still undone, and who are in the full
vigour of life, who indeed would probably prefer to
-enter the Academy by the door of examination than by
the door of election. It would be absurd to submit a
man of 65 to a literary examination. If you deem him
worthy of Academic horiours, bestow them on him with
gaod grace, if not, leave him die in peace. The same
holds true of men much younger. It would not be
easy to fix an age limit above which examination would
be unseemly ; but that there is such a limit, varying no

doubt with the individual and the circumstances of

each case, it is impossible to deny. On the other hand
it would be absurd to crown a young man who may have
written a clever book or two, to crown him as an Aca-
demician. Such an individual should rather be en-
couraged to do still better work and to conquer his way
o the Academy by unquestionable merits and achieve-
‘ments, B

The Academy should be such a body that every indi-
vidual composing it would be a recognised authority on
the Irish language and literature, so that its collective
authority could not be gainsaid in the literary and
Tlearned world. Tt is not, of cowrse, to be expccbed that
every member should know overybhing about every
branch of Irish learning, but every member should be
an authority in his own sphere. An Academy properly
chosen and setting to work seriously, would be expected
%o do much for the Irish language. ~An idea entertained
by some that _its chief work would be to * settle the
spelling ’* of Irish secms to me ridiculous. The spelling
dually getting settled by the mere fact
that so much of it is getting printed. The differenco in

_ spelling now hetween oducated Irvish writers is incon-

THE LEADER.

ol

siderable and hardly wider than the latitude allowable
in othor languages. ~ Nor is the invention of a technical
phraseology for the arts and sciences of such imme-
diate practical necessity as to engage the serious atfen-
tion at present of an Academy.

‘What is most wanted, I believe, is to elevate modern
Irish, prose and verse, to something like the dignity
which should belong to the national literature of the
Gael if it had been allowed to develop freely during the
past fow centuries. It is not a matter of surprise that
the taste of Irish writers of the present day has sunk
rather low in some instances, that the spirit of some-
thing in its own way as low as the stage Irishman is an
inspiring force in many modern productions in Irish,
that a fatal fondness for rubbishy folk-lore as class
literature has seized on teachers and pupils alike. Folk-
lore has its own sphere, but that sphere does not extend
to class books for children, or to the region of polite
literature. The Intermediate Board and the Royal
University are doing nothing to check this pernicious
taste ; on the contrary they rather encourage it. Indeced
the texts generally prescribed by the latter body are
scarcely modern Irish af all. Many of them might have
been written by Fionn MacCumhail. As for the In-
termediate texts of late some of them leave a bad taste
in the mouth, and some of them are prescribed before
they are printed. To give Irish literature a lift then in
this and in other directions, is one of the most impor-
tant works which an Irish Academy could undertake.
They should set about it by precept and example, hence
the importance of insisting on confining such an Aca-
demy to men of acknowledged distinction as writers and
of admitted general learning and culture. If Irish
literature is to consist of stories about tailors and pigs
and the like interesting animals, it will be justly de-
rided and decried not only by a hostile public, but by
litterateurs the world over. Tt is time for Irish litera-
ture to look up. Hitherto it has been groovy and frag-
mentary. It needs models and encouragement, it needs
to be kept pure and wholesome. At the present time a
large amount of what would be considered slang in a
normal state of things passes with some for idiom, and if
our language continues to be handled by persons whose
knowledge of it, such as it is, came to them when they
were already grown up, and who, moreover, lack culti-
vated minds and trained judgments, the general run of
our rising literature will deserve to wear the badge of
inferiority and vulgarity. It is necessary, too, to keep
our language as free as possible from the contamination
of English habits of thought and expression. Absolute
freedom from English words and English idioms is an
impossibility, and has been impossible for some centuries,
Nor is absolute freedom desirable.  Every cultivated
language borrows from its neighbours, often to its own
advantage. But it is necessary that the warp and the
woof of all we write and say should be Irish. English
idioms and words that have crept into the ordinary con-
versation of the people, particularly in certain districts,
should be carefully elimated by writers who are anxious
to consult the dignity of the Irish language and of Irish
litetature. The grammatical, philological, lexicographi-
cal,”and other aspects of the language, too, claim eon-
sideration from an Aocademy.

But I maintain that the time has not yot come for
launching an Institution of the kind, and that the
chances of its succeeding at present are oxceedingly
small. The way can, however, be paved for it by a scheme
of progressive examinations, of which only a faint shadow
has been so far put before the public. An Academy, if
it be called into existence, should be self-legislating,
self-subsisting, and not a set of ornamental fossils used
for show by an outside committee. It should be borne
in mind in dealing with this matter that the governing
body of the Gaelic League which, it is proposed, is to
call this body into existence, has done good work in the
past for the publication of Irish literature, original and
cdited, and that it is hoped that the lull that exists at
present in its output is only passing. Indeed, in the
quantity of its Irish publications it perhaps stands in
the front vank.
